root_text.sn45.7:1.2: Atha kho aññataro bhikkhu yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami …pe… ekamantaṁ nisinno kho so bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn45.7:2.2: Kissa nu kho etaṁ, bhante, adhivacanaṁ:
root_text.sn45.7:3.1: Evaṁ vutte, so bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn45.7:3.3: Katamaṁ nu kho, bhante, amataṁ, katamo amatagāmimaggo”ti?
root_text.sn45.7:3.6: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo amatagāmimaggo, seyyathidaṁ—

translation_text.sn45.7:1.2: Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and said to him:
translation_text.sn45.7:2.1: “Sir, they speak of ‘the removal of greed, hate, and delusion’.
translation_text.sn45.7:2.2: What is this a term for?”
translation_text.sn45.7:2.4: “Mendicant, the removal of greed, hate, and delusion is a term for the element of extinguishment.
translation_text.sn45.7:2.6: It’s used to speak of the ending of defilements.”
translation_text.sn45.7:3.1: When he said this, the mendicant said to the Buddha:
translation_text.sn45.7:3.3: What is freedom from death? And what is the path that leads to freedom from death?”
translation_text.sn45.7:3.4: “The ending of greed, hate, and delusion.
translation_text.sn45.7:3.5: This is called freedom from death.
translation_text.sn45.7:3.6: The path that leads to freedom from death is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn45.7:3.7: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.”
